chunk_summary: This section discusses practical strategies for implementing a gluten-free diet (GFD) in Indian households, focusing on cross-contact prevention, nutritional adequacy, and addressing deficiencies in celiac disease management.
learning_objectives: Understand the challenges of implementing a gluten-free diet in Indian households with shared kitchens.
Learn strategies to prevent cross-contact with gluten in daily food preparation.
Identify key nutritional deficiencies in celiac disease and develop supplementation plans.
Recognize the importance of monitoring growth and symptoms to assess adherence to a gluten-free diet.
key_facts: A gluten-free diet (GFD) is the only disease-modifying therapy for celiac disease.
Cross-contact in shared kitchens is a major cause of persistent anemia and partial response in Indian households.
Practical measures include separate storage, dedicated utensils, and avoiding shared oil or sieves for gluten-free cooking.
Reliance on ultra-processed gluten-free products can reduce fiber and micronutrient intake and increase costs.
Persistent anemia, poor height velocity, or delayed puberty should prompt a review of cross-contact sources rather than expanding food eliminations.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: A gluten-free diet (GFD) is the only disease-modifying therapy for celiac disease, but success depends on household implementation rather than intent.
document_key_topics: Gluten-free diet implementation in Indian households
Nutritional adequacy and supplementation in celiac disease
